title: Cat Ear Canal Swollen Shut: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's ear canal swollen shut? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why prompt veterinary care is essential for relief and hearing.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat's ear canal is swollen shut, it is typically a condition called chronic otitis externa or proliferative tissue change. This occurs when the skin lining the ear canal becomes so inflamed and thickened that the opening narrows or completely closes. Common causes include long-term ear infections, severe allergies, ear mites, or inflammatory polyps. Because the canal is closed, wax and discharge become trapped, often leading to a secondary, deep-seated infection.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. While a swollen ear canal is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a source of significant pain and discomfort for your cat. If left untreated, the infection can migrate to the middle or inner ear, potentially causing permanent hearing loss, balance issues, or a ruptured eardrum. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to begin professional treatment and pain management.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected ear can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows them to see the severity of the swelling, the color of any visible discharge, and whether there are any growths or foreign bodies near the opening. This visual information helps the clinic determine how quickly your cat needs to be squeezed into the schedule and allows them to prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter drops to open the ear canal?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid putting any medications or cleaners into a swollen ear canal unless directed by a vet. If the eardrum is ruptured behind the swelling, certain medications can be toxic to the inner ear and cause permanent damage.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do for a swollen ear?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam, take a swab for cytology to identify the infection type, and may prescribe anti-inflammatory steroids to reduce the swelling alongside antibiotics or antifungals.

faqs.question: Does this condition require surgery?
faqs.answer: In some chronic cases where the ear canal has become permanently thickened and 'mineralized,' surgery (such as a TECA) may be required. However, many cases can be managed with aggressive medical therapy if caught early enough.
